nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to Lake Matano in the Malili lake system of central Sulawesi, where it is found among rocks and underwater cliffs from shallow water down to about 10 m. The lake is ultra-oligotrophic — meaning extremely low in dissolved nutrients — with exceptionally stable, warm, and mildly alkaline water year-round.

decorPreferences: smooth rocks and rubble (Lake Matano-style hardscape)

diet.notes: In the wild, Cardinal Sulawesi Shrimp graze primarily on biofilm, algae, and decaying organic matter. In captivity they do well on small amounts of high-quality powdered shrimp foods (such as spirulina or snowflake-style biofilm powders), occasional blanched vegetables, and bacterial supplements. Portions should be kept small, as overfeeding fouls the water and is a leading cause of colony loss.

goodWith: other Sulawesi Caridina (C. spinata, C. woltereckae)
avoidWith: fish (almost any will harass or eat them)

summary: A small, deep-red shrimp dotted with brilliant white spots, endemic to Lake Matano in Sulawesi. A demanding specialist requiring warm, hard, alkaline water and a species-only tank.
careNotes: Cardinal Sulawesi Shrimp are among the most demanding dwarf shrimp in the hobby and are generally advised for experienced keepers only. They evolved in the unusually warm, mildly alkaline, and parameter-stable waters of Lake Matano (Sulawesi, Indonesia), and tend to tolerate very little deviation from those conditions. Most keepers run remineralized RO/DI water with a Sulawesi-specific mineral salt (e.g., Salty Shrimp Sulawesi Mineral 7.5/8.5) targeting roughly TDS 100–160 ppm, pH 7.8–8.4, GH 4–8, KH 4–8, and 27–30°C. A species-only tank is strongly recommended: almost any fish will harass or eat them, and they compete poorly even with peaceful tankmates. Avoid copper-based medications entirely — like most freshwater invertebrates, they are acutely sensitive to copper. The species is listed as Critically Endangered (possibly extinct in the wild) on the IUCN Red List, so almost all hobby stock comes from captive breeding; supporting reputable breeders is preferred over wild-collected animals. The tank must be fully cycled and stable before any shrimp are introduced, as ammonia and nitrite are acutely toxic to them. Acclimation should be slow drip over several hours given the parameter gap between most shipping water and a target Sulawesi tank.
breedingNotes: Breeding is possible in captivity but generally rated moderate-to-difficult due to slow reproduction and parameter sensitivity. Females carry roughly 15–30 reddish eggs under the swimmerets, with an incubation period commonly reported at 20–28 days. Larvae develop directly into fully-formed miniature shrimp in freshwater (no brackish larval stage required), but juvenile survival is highly dependent on stable water parameters, abundant biofilm, and the absence of predators. A dedicated species-only tank with mature biofilm, gentle sponge filtration, and minimal disturbance gives the best results. Sexing is difficult without close inspection or infrared lighting; females tend to be slightly larger with a deeper abdomen.

primary.name: Aquarium Breeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder)
primary.notes: Primary swap: assigned primary Flip Aquatics (https://flipaquatics.com/collections/sulawesi-shrimp) returned HTTP 404, and assigned secondary The Shrimp Farm (https://www.theshrimpfarm.com/) returned HTTP 403. A dedicated species care guide on aquariumbreeder.com provided the most complete and consistent dataset across all required fields, so it was promoted to primary.

additional.name: Aquarium Source
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~1 inch), temperature (77–86°F preferred ~82°F), pH (7.8–8.2), gH (6–8), tank size minimum 5–10 gal, species-only/copper-sensitive guidance, and biotope description (Lake Matano, Malili system).

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature 77–88°F, pH 7.0–8.5 (8.0 ideal), kH 3–10 dKH, tank size ~5 gal floor, lifespan up to 2 years, peaceful temperament, copper sensitivity, and that the species occurs in two ancient lakes (note: most sources restrict it to Lake Matano specifically).

additional.name: Wikipedia (Caridina dennerli)
additional.notes: Taxonomic confirmation: Atyidae, described by Von Rintelen & Cai (2009). Endemic to Lake Matano, found among rocks and cliffs from shallow water to ~10 m. Critically Endangered, possibly extinct in the wild (IUCN). Species named for the German company Dennerle. Confirms coloration: dark red/purple body with conspicuous small white spots, white antennae and partly white snout.

additional.name: GBIF — Caridina dennerli von Rintelen & Cai, 2009
additional.notes: Taxonomic backbone confirmation: family Atyidae, order Decapoda. Used to lock taxonomy fields.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Like other dwarf shrimp, the Cardinal Sulawesi Shrimp molts periodically as it grows, shedding its exoskeleton to allow for growth. Caves, rocks, and rubble should be provided so the animal can shelter during the soft, vulnerable post-molt period. Sudden swings in water parameters can trigger failed molts, a condition sometimes called 'White Ring of Death' by hobbyists. Keeping GH stable and using a Sulawesi-specific remineraliser ensures adequate calcium and magnesium for successful shell formation.
crustacean.breedingNotes: The species develops directly in freshwater with no brackish larval stage required. Females carry roughly 15 to 30 eggs under their swimmerets for approximately 20 to 28 days, after which fully formed miniature shrimp emerge. Breeding is only likely to succeed in a stable, well-cycled tank maintained at Sulawesi parameters, with abundant biofilm covering surfaces and no predators present to pick off juveniles.
